meta.case: Meridian Retail Group plc  v  TechFlow Solutions Ltd

nodes.label: P1
nodes.text: The Claimant is a public limited company operating approximately 180 retail stores in the United Kingdom and an online sales channel.

nodes.label: The Claimant is a public limited company o
nodes.fulltext: The Claimant is a public limited company operating approximately 180 retail stores in the United Kingdom and an online sales channel.
nodes.quote: The Claimant ("Meridian") is a public limited company and operates a chain of approximately 180 retail stores in the United Kingdom together with an online sales channel.

nodes.label: Helena Vance identifies the claimant as 'M
nodes.fulltext: Helena Vance identifies the claimant as 'Meridian Retail Group plc', which supports only the 'public limited company' element of the claim, but says nothing about store numbers or online sales.
nodes.quote: B E T W E E N : MERIDIAN RETAIL GROUP PLC Claimant - and - TECHFLOW SOLUTIONS LIMITED Defendant

nodes.label: Raymond Okafor says he is responsible for
nodes.fulltext: Raymond Okafor says he is responsible for the running of Meridian's 'store estate' and refers to around 40 stores affected by stock shortages, which implies a multi-store retail business in the UK but does not prove approximately 180 stores or an online channel.
nodes.quote: I am responsible for the running of our store estate.

nodes.label: The forensic accountant analyses EPOS sale
nodes.fulltext: The forensic accountant analyses EPOS sales data and retail trading performance of the claimant, which is consistent with a retail business, but does not address plc status, store count, UK footprint, or online sales channel.
nodes.quote: B E T W E E N : MERIDIAN RETAIL GROUP PLC Claimant - and - TECHFLOW SOLUTIONS LIMITED Defendant

nodes.label: P2
nodes.text: The Defendant is a limited company which designs, supplies and implements retail software systems.

nodes.label: The Defendant is a limited company which d
nodes.fulltext: The Defendant is a limited company which designs, supplies and implements retail software systems.
nodes.quote: The Defendant ("TechFlow") is a limited company which designs, supplies and implements retail software systems.

nodes.label: The Master Services Agreement identifies t
nodes.fulltext: The Master Services Agreement identifies the Defendant as 'TECHFLOW SOLUTIONS LIMITED' and as the 'Supplier', and states that it shall 'design, build, configure, test and implement' a cloud-based retail inventory and EPOS platform.
nodes.quote: 1 The Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform ("the Platform") in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW").

nodes.label: The Statement of Work, forming Schedule 1
nodes.fulltext: The Statement of Work, forming Schedule 1 to the MSA, confirms the retail nature of the system by defining Phase 1 as 'core inventory and EPOS' and Phase 2 as 'reporting and analytics', supporting that the Defendant supplies and implements retail software systems.
nodes.quote: 1 The Supplier shall deliver the Platform in two phases: Phase 1 (core inventory and EPOS); Phase 2 (reporting and analytics).

nodes.label: The witness statement names 'TECHFLOW SOLU
nodes.fulltext: The witness statement names 'TECHFLOW SOLUTIONS LIMITED' as Defendant and describes Helena Vance working closely with TechFlow on the platform implementation programme, consistent with TechFlow implementing the retail platform.
nodes.quote: B E T W E E N : MERIDIAN RETAIL GROUP PLC Claimant - and - TECHFLOW SOLUTIONS LIMITED Defendant

nodes.label: P3
nodes.text: By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024, the Defendant agreed to design, build, configure and implement for the Claimant a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform.

nodes.label: By a written Master Services Agreement dat
nodes.fulltext: By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024, the Defendant agreed to design, build, configure and implement for the Claimant a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform.
nodes.quote: By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 ("the MSA"), TechFlow agreed to design, build, configure and implement for Meridian a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform

nodes.label: The written Master Services Agreement date
nodes.fulltext: The written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 identifies the parties and expressly provides at clause 1.1 that the Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform for the Customer.
nodes.quote: 1 The Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform ("the Platform") in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW").

nodes.label: Schedule 1 to the MSA dated 14 March 2024
nodes.fulltext: Schedule 1 to the MSA dated 14 March 2024 elaborates the agreed scope, describing delivery of the Platform in phases and confirming it is the inventory and EPOS platform contemplated by the MSA.
nodes.quote: SCHEDULE 1 — STATEMENT OF WORK (SOW-01) to the Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 1.

nodes.label: The Order Form dated 20 March 2024 is expr
nodes.fulltext: The Order Form dated 20 March 2024 is expressly made under the Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 and refers to platform licence and implementation for core inventory and EPOS, reinforcing the existence and subject matter of the agreement.
nodes.quote: ORDER FORM Order Ref: MRG-TF-OF-001 | Dated: 20 March 2024 Customer: Meridian Retail Group plc Supplier: TechFlow Solutions Limited Under: Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024

nodes.label: The termination letter refers to the same
nodes.fulltext: The termination letter refers to the same Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 and to TechFlow’s design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform under that agreement, corroborating the pleaded contractual arrangement.
nodes.quote: We write in connection with your design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform under the above Agreement ("the MSA").

nodes.label: The witness statement is consistent with t
nodes.fulltext: The witness statement is consistent with the existence of the original MSA/SOW framework for the platform implementation and later changes under that contract, supporting the underlying contractual relationship.
nodes.quote: Statement of Truth I believe that the facts stated in this witness statement are true.

nodes.label: P4
nodes.text: The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work at Schedule 1 setting out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.

nodes.label: The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work a
nodes.fulltext: The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work at Schedule 1 setting out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.
nodes.quote: The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work ("the SOW") at Schedule 1, which set out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.

nodes.label: The MSA expressly states at clause 1.1 tha
nodes.fulltext: The MSA expressly states at clause 1.1 that the Supplier will perform the services in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1, and clauses 2.1 and 3.1 refer to milestones and the timetable stated in the SOW.
nodes.quote: 1 The Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform ("the Platform") in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW").

nodes.label: This document is expressly headed 'SCHEDUL
nodes.fulltext: This document is expressly headed 'SCHEDULE 1 — STATEMENT OF WORK' to the MSA dated 14 March 2024 and sets out scope, implementation timetable/plan, and milestones M1-M4.
nodes.quote: SCHEDULE 1 — STATEMENT OF WORK (SOW-01) to the Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 1.

nodes.label: The deed of variation refers to clause 4.1
nodes.fulltext: The deed of variation refers to clause 4.1 of the SOW and says it varies only payment milestones while leaving scope and target go-live unchanged, reinforcing that the SOW formed part of the MSA framework.
nodes.quote: This Deed does not vary the scope of the works or the target go-live date.

nodes.label: The order form is made under the MSA and r
nodes.fulltext: The order form is made under the MSA and refers to payment terms on milestones M1-M4 per the SOW, corroborating that the SOW contained milestone provisions used within the contractual structure.
nodes.quote: Payment terms: 25% on each milestone M1–M4 per the SOW.

nodes.label: P5
nodes.text: The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 excluding VAT, payable against milestones.

nodes.label: The total charges payable under the MSA we
nodes.fulltext: The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 excluding VAT, payable against milestones.
nodes.quote: The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against milestones.

nodes.label: Clause 2.1 of the MSA expressly states tha
nodes.fulltext: Clause 2.1 of the MSA expressly states that total charges for the Services are £2,400,000 excluding VAT, payable against the milestones set out in the SOW, subject to written variation.
nodes.quote: 1 The total charges for the Services are £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against the milestones set out in the SOW and as may be varied by agreement in writing.

nodes.label: The Order Form under the MSA repeats a tot
nodes.fulltext: The Order Form under the MSA repeats a total of £2,400,000 excluding VAT and states payment terms of 25% on each milestone M1–M4 per the SOW.
nodes.quote: Payment terms: 25% on each milestone M1–M4 per the SOW.

nodes.label: The SOW sets out milestone-based payment m
nodes.fulltext: The SOW sets out milestone-based payment mechanics, originally 25% on each of M1, M2, M3 and M4.
nodes.quote: 1 Charges are payable 25% on each of M1, M2, M3 and M4, subject to any variation agreed under the change control procedure.

nodes.label: The Deed of Variation changes the payment
nodes.fulltext: The Deed of Variation changes the payment milestone profile from 25/25/25/25 to 20/20/30/30. This does not alter the total price but means the pleaded wording may overstate the original milestone profile if read as unchanged throughout.
nodes.quote: 1 of the SOW is varied so that the charges are payable: 20% on M1, 20% on M2, 30% on M3 and 30% on M4.

nodes.label: Change Order No. 3 adds an additional char
nodes.fulltext: Change Order No. 3 adds an additional charge of £180,000 payable on go-live for the Loyalty Module, showing that the total charges under the contractual arrangements were later increased beyond the original £2,400,000.
nodes.quote: 1 The additional charge for the Loyalty Module is £180,000, payable on go-live.

nodes.label: P6
nodes.text: It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024.

nodes.label: It was an express term of the MSA and the
nodes.fulltext: It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024.
nodes.quote: It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024

nodes.label: The SOW lists milestone M4 as 'Go-live (ta
nodes.fulltext: The SOW lists milestone M4 as 'Go-live (target date) 1 October 2024', so it does contain 1 October 2024 as the original contractual date in the timetable.
nodes.quote: 1 The go-live date of 1 October 2024 is a target date.

nodes.label: The MSA refers to the target go-live date
nodes.fulltext: The MSA refers to the target go-live date stated in the SOW, confirming the date was part of the contractual framework, but not as a fixed date.
nodes.quote: 1 The Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date stated in the SOW.

nodes.label: Change Order No. 3 expressly revises the g
nodes.fulltext: Change Order No. 3 expressly revises the go-live date from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024 for the Platform as a whole, reinforcing that the date was not fixed and was capable of contractual variation.
nodes.quote: 1 The parties agree that, to accommodate the additional works, the go-live date is revised from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024.

nodes.label: The October 2024 email thread discusses pr
nodes.fulltext: The October 2024 email thread discusses proceeding to go-live on 18 November 2024 and even a possible deferral to January 2025, consistent with a movable target date rather than a fixed contractual deadline.
nodes.quote: EMAIL CORRESPONDENCE Thread: Go-live decision From: Marcus Reilly <m.

nodes.label: P7
nodes.text: It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that time was of the essence in respect of the 1 October 2024 go-live date.

nodes.label: It was an express term of the MSA and the
nodes.fulltext: It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that time was of the essence in respect of the 1 October 2024 go-live date.
nodes.quote: and that time was of the essence in respect of that date.

nodes.label: The SOW expressly states that the 1 Octobe
nodes.fulltext: The SOW expressly states that the 1 October 2024 go-live is a 'target date' and that the implementation plan is a planning estimate subject to the MSA change control procedure.
nodes.quote: The detailed implementation plan is a planning estimate and is subject to the change control procedure in clause 3 of the MSA.

nodes.label: The MSA provides only that the Supplier sh
nodes.fulltext: The MSA provides only that the Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date in the SOW, and acknowledges the timetable is a planning estimate adjustable through change control.
nodes.quote: 1 The Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date stated in the SOW.

nodes.label: The signed Change Order revised the go-liv
nodes.fulltext: The signed Change Order revised the go-live date from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024, reinforcing that the original date was not fixed or treated as immutable.
nodes.quote: 1 The parties agree that, to accommodate the additional works, the go-live date is revised from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024.

nodes.label: The parties' email correspondence accepted
nodes.fulltext: The parties' email correspondence accepted that adding the loyalty module could move go-live from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024, inconsistent with any express 'time of the essence' term for 1 October.
nodes.quote: The additional cost is £180,000 and, given the additional build and test, we will need to move go-live from 1 October to 18 November 2024.

nodes.label: Later correspondence discusses deferring g
nodes.fulltext: Later correspondence discusses deferring go-live beyond 18 November and Meridian choosing to proceed despite risk, again consistent with a movable target date rather than an essential fixed deadline.
nodes.quote: Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned.

nodes.label: P8
nodes.text: At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, before the MSA was entered into, the Defendant's Sales Director orally represented that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions.

nodes.label: At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024,
nodes.fulltext: At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, before the MSA was entered into, the Defendant's Sales Director orally represented that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions.
nodes.quote: At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, prior to entering into the MSA, TechFlow by its Sales Director Mr Daniel Frost orally represented to Meridian that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions

nodes.label: The Statement of Work dated 14 March 2024
nodes.fulltext: The Statement of Work dated 14 March 2024 sets out scope, milestones and responsibilities, but contains no reference to any pre-contract oral representation, any meeting on or about 5 February 2024, any Sales Director, or any performance assurance of 10,000 concurrent transactions.
nodes.quote: SCHEDULE 1 — STATEMENT OF WORK (SOW-01) to the Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 1.

nodes.label: Helena Vance's witness statement addresses
nodes.fulltext: Helena Vance's witness statement addresses later 2024 change requests, go-live decisions and post-go-live issues, but does not mention any February 2024 meeting, any oral representation by a Sales Director, or any concurrency-capacity statement.
nodes.quote: WITNESS STATEMENT OF HELENA VANCE 1.

nodes.label: Priya Nair's witness statement confirms si
nodes.fulltext: Priya Nair's witness statement confirms signing the MSA on 14 March 2024 and later contractual steps, but gives no evidence of a pre-contract meeting on 5 February 2024 or of any oral representation about supporting 10,000 concurrent transactions.
nodes.quote: WITNESS STATEMENT OF PRIYA NAIR 1.

nodes.label: The defendant solicitor's response dispute
nodes.fulltext: The defendant solicitor's response disputes liability under the March 2024 MSA and discusses later events only; it does not support the alleged February 2024 oral representation and, by omission, leaves the pleaded pre-contract statement uncorroborated in this evidence set.
nodes.quote: Caldwell Pryce LLP 18 Bishopsgate, London EC2N 4AJ 7 February 2025 Dear Sirs TechFlow Solutions Limited – Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 – RESPONSE TO LETTER BEFORE ACTION We act for TechFlow Solutions Limited.

nodes.label: The defect log concerns incidents from Nov
nodes.fulltext: The defect log concerns incidents from November 2024 to January 2025 and says nothing about any pre-contract representation or tested concurrency capacity of 10,000 transactions.
nodes.quote: PLATFORM DEFECT / ISSUE LOG Extract — 18 November 2024 to 10 January 2025 Ref Date raised Severity Description Status / cause D-001 19 Nov 2024 Sev 1 Stock-sync: tills show incorrect stock counts; oversell on promotional lines Platform defect — fixed 31 Dec 2024 D-002 22 Nov 2024 Sev 1 Stock-sync: overnight reconciliation job fails intermittently Platform defect — fixed 2 Jan 2025 D-003 25 Nov 2024 Sev 1 Total loss of EPOS connectivity, all stores, ~5 hours Network (Northgate Telecom) — not Platform D-004 29 Nov 2024 Sev 3 Receipt printer formatting incorrect on loyalty redemptions Platform defect — fixed 6 Dec 2024 D-005 2 Dec 2024 Sev 4 Report export to CSV mis-orders columns Platform defect — fixed 9 Dec 2024 D-006 11 Dec 2024 Sev 2 Intermittent till freezes traced to in-store broadband dropouts Store network — not Platform Note: Recorded Platform unavailability attributable to Platform defects over the period was modest; the single largest outage (D-003) and the intermittent till freezes (D-006) were attributable to network connectivity provided by Meridian, not to the Platform.

nodes.label: P9
nodes.text: The Claimant entered into the MSA in reliance upon that representation.

nodes.label: The Claimant entered into the MSA in relia
nodes.fulltext: The Claimant entered into the MSA in reliance upon that representation.
nodes.quote: Meridian entered into the MSA in reliance upon the Representation.

nodes.label: Priya Nair says she signed the MSA on 14 M
nodes.fulltext: Priya Nair says she signed the MSA on 14 March 2024, proving entry into the contract, but she does not say Meridian relied on any pre-contract representation when doing so.
nodes.quote: I signed the Master Services Agreement on Meridian’s behalf on 14 March 2024, and the related Order Form.

nodes.label: The termination letter advances Meridian's
nodes.fulltext: The termination letter advances Meridian's complaint about later breaches and defects under the MSA, but it does not identify any pre-contract representation inducing entry into the MSA.
nodes.quote: We write in connection with your design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform under the above Agreement ("the MSA").

nodes.label: The MSA contains an entire agreement and n
nodes.fulltext: The MSA contains an entire agreement and non-reliance clause: each party acknowledges it has not relied on any representation or statement not expressly set out in the agreement (save fraud). That directly undermines a pleaded reliance case unless the alleged representation is contractual or fraudulent.
nodes.quote: 2 Each party acknowledges that it has not relied on, and shall have no remedy in respect of, any representation or statement not expressly set out in this Agreement (save in the case of fraud).

nodes.label: TechFlow's response letter denies liabilit
nodes.fulltext: TechFlow's response letter denies liability and relies on the contractual framework; while not direct evidence of formation, it is consistent with the written contract governing the parties' rights rather than any extra-contractual inducement.
nodes.quote: Caldwell Pryce LLP 18 Bishopsgate, London EC2N 4AJ 7 February 2025 Dear Sirs TechFlow Solutions Limited – Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 – RESPONSE TO LETTER BEFORE ACTION We act for TechFlow Solutions Limited.

nodes.text: The representation that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions was false.

nodes.label: The representation that the Platform would

nodes.quote: The Representation was false

nodes.label: Expert report analyses availability, outag
nodes.fulltext: Expert report analyses availability, outages and defects, but says nothing about any representation or testing threshold of 10,000 concurrent transactions; it instead attributes major outages largely to network issues and identifies different defects.
nodes.quote: Defects 5.

nodes.label: Defect log records stock-sync defects and
nodes.fulltext: Defect log records stock-sync defects and some outages, with note that platform-attributable unavailability was modest; it contains no evidence about a 10,000 concurrent transaction capacity representation or failure of such capacity.

nodes.label: Witness statement describes stock-sync and
nodes.fulltext: Witness statement describes stock-sync and connectivity problems after go-live, but does not address any representation about supporting 10,000 concurrent transactions.
nodes.quote: After go-live there were real problems with the stock-synchronisation module.

nodes.label: Witness statement acknowledges operational
nodes.fulltext: Witness statement acknowledges operational problems and non-platform causes of outages, but gives no evidence of a 10,000 concurrent transaction representation or that such a capacity claim was false.
nodes.quote: Statement of Truth I believe that the facts stated in this witness statement are true.

nodes.label: Statement of Work defines scope, timetable
nodes.fulltext: Statement of Work defines scope, timetable and customer network responsibilities, but contains no term or representation about reliably supporting at least 10,000 concurrent transactions.

nodes.label: P11
nodes.text: The Platform was incapable of supporting at least 10,000 concurrent transactions.

nodes.label: The Platform was incapable of supporting a
nodes.fulltext: The Platform was incapable of supporting at least 10,000 concurrent transactions.
nodes.quote: the Platform was incapable of supporting that volume.

nodes.label: Expert report analyses platform/applicatio
nodes.fulltext: Expert report analyses platform/application/database logs and addresses availability and defects, but gives no finding that the Platform lacked capacity for 10,000 concurrent transactions; instead it focuses on modest platform-attributable unavailability and network-caused outages, undermining any attempt to infer a concurrency-capacity failure from outage evidence.
nodes.quote: Availability of the Platform 2.

nodes.label: Defect log records stock-sync defects, rep
nodes.fulltext: Defect log records stock-sync defects, reporting/printing issues, and notes that the largest outage and some freezes were network-related, not Platform-caused. It contains no evidence of load testing, transaction-capacity limits, or inability to handle 10,000 concurrent transactions.
nodes.quote: PLATFORM DEFECT / ISSUE LOG Extract — 18 November 2024 to 10 January 2025 Ref Date raised Severity Description Status / cause D-001 19 Nov 2024 Sev 1 Stock-sync: tills show incorrect stock counts; oversell on promotional lines Platform defect — fixed 31 Dec 2024 D-002 22 Nov 2024 Sev 1 Stock-sync: overnight reconciliation job fails intermittently Platform defect — fixed 2 Jan 2025 D-003 25 Nov 2024 Sev 1 Total loss of EPOS connectivity, all stores, ~5 hours Network (Northgate Telecom) — not Platform D-004 29 Nov 2024 Sev 3 Receipt printer formatting incorrect on loyalty redemptions Platform defect — fixed 6 Dec 2024 D-005 2 Dec 2024 Sev 4 Report export to CSV mis-orders columns Platform defect — fixed 9 Dec 2024 D-006 11 Dec 2024 Sev 2 Intermittent till freezes traced to in-store broadband dropouts Store network — not Platform Note: Recorded Platform unavailability attributable to Platform defects over the period was modest; the single largest outage (D-003) and the intermittent till freezes (D-006) were attributable to network connectivity provided by Meridian, not to the Platform.

nodes.label: Root-cause email states the major 25 Novem
nodes.fulltext: Root-cause email states the major 25 November outage was caused by WAN/network failure while the Platform application and database remained healthy. That tends to negate any inference that the outage demonstrates insufficient transaction concurrency capacity.
nodes.quote: EMAIL CORRESPONDENCE Thread: 25 November outage — root cause From: Sanjay Patel <s.

nodes.label: Witness statement accepts stock-sync probl
nodes.fulltext: Witness statement accepts stock-sync problems but says several outages were due to broadband/network failures rather than the Platform. It provides no evidence about transaction concurrency thresholds or inability to support 10,000 concurrent transactions.
nodes.quote: None of this is to say the platform problems did not hurt us — they did, especially the stock-sync issues — but they were one factor among several.

nodes.label: P12
nodes.text: In breach of the MSA, the Defendant delivered the Platform late.

nodes.label: In breach of the MSA, the Defendant delive
nodes.fulltext: In breach of the MSA, the Defendant delivered the Platform late.
nodes.quote: In breach of the MSA, TechFlow delivered the Platform late.

nodes.label: Meridian's termination letter asserts that
nodes.fulltext: Meridian's termination letter asserts that TechFlow 'delivered the Platform late' and refers to go-live on 18 November 2024.
nodes.quote: In particular: (i) you delivered the Platform late; (ii) following go-live on 18 November 2024 the Platform suffered serious and repeated failures, including critical defects in the stock-synchronisation module; and (iii) the Platform was not of satisfactory quality nor fit for purpose.

nodes.label: The SOW shows an original go-live target d
nodes.fulltext: The SOW shows an original go-live target date of 1 October 2024, so delivery by 18 November 2024 was later than the original plan in a chronological sense.
nodes.quote: 1 The go-live date of 1 October 2024 is a target date.

nodes.label: Helena Vance says go-live was moved to 18
nodes.fulltext: Helena Vance says go-live was moved to 18 November 2024 and that the platform did in fact go live then, which helps establish chronology.
nodes.quote: I decided that we should go live on 18 November 2024.

nodes.label: TechFlow's response directly denies late d
nodes.fulltext: TechFlow's response directly denies late delivery, relying on the target-date wording and the signed Change Order moving go-live from 1 October to 18 November 2024.
nodes.quote: It moved from 1 October to 18 November 2024 by reason of Change Order No.

nodes.label: P13
nodes.text: The Platform did not go live until 18 November 2024, about seven weeks after the contractual go-live date.

nodes.label: The Platform did not go live until 18 Nove
nodes.fulltext: The Platform did not go live until 18 November 2024, about seven weeks after the contractual go-live date.
nodes.quote: The Platform did not go live until 18 November 2024, some seven weeks after the contractual go-live date.

nodes.label: Email thread dated 24 October 2024 records
nodes.fulltext: Email thread dated 24 October 2024 records TechFlow recommending deferral to January 2025 but stating it could proceed with go-live on 18 November 2024, and Meridian instructing TechFlow to proceed on 18 November 2024 as planned.
nodes.quote: Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned.

nodes.label: Helena Vance’s witness statement expressly
nodes.fulltext: Helena Vance’s witness statement expressly says Change Order No. 3 moved the go-live date to 18 November 2024 and that she decided the platform should go live on 18 November 2024.

nodes.label: Defect log runs from 18 November 2024 onwa
nodes.fulltext: Defect log runs from 18 November 2024 onward and records issues raised from 19 November 2024, which is consistent with the platform having gone live on or about 18 November 2024.

nodes.label: The Statement of Work gives 1 October 2024
nodes.fulltext: The Statement of Work gives 1 October 2024 only as a 'target date' and says the implementation timetable is a planning estimate subject to change control, so describing it as the contractual go-live date overstates the contractual position.
nodes.quote: 1 The go-live date of 1 October 2024 is a target date.

nodes.label: The UAT acceptance certificate dated 12 No
nodes.fulltext: The UAT acceptance certificate dated 12 November 2024 shows Phase 1 had passed UAT before 18 November 2024, which does not contradict go-live on 18 November but shows the system was accepted before then and does not itself prove the exact live date.
nodes.quote: PHASE 1 — USER ACCEPTANCE TESTING ACCEPTANCE CERTIFICATE Project: Meridian Platform implementation (MSA 14 March 2024) Certificate ref: UAT-P1-CERT Date: 12 November 2024

nodes.quote: Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works.

nodes.label: Direct email from Helena Vance requests a
nodes.fulltext: Direct email from Helena Vance requests a new customer loyalty module, expressly acknowledging it was not in the original SOW and asking TechFlow to scope it as a change, with possible timetable impact.
nodes.quote: EMAIL CORRESPONDENCE Thread: New requirement — loyalty module From: Helena Vance <h.

nodes.label: Claimant witness Helena Vance admits that

nodes.label: P27
nodes.text: The Defendant failed to provide adequate training to the Claimant's staff in the use of the Platform.

nodes.label: The Defendant failed to provide adequate t
nodes.fulltext: The Defendant failed to provide adequate training to the Claimant's staff in the use of the Platform.
nodes.quote: Further, TechFlow failed to provide adequate training to Meridian’s staff in the use of the Platform.

nodes.label: The Statement of Work expressly provides t
nodes.fulltext: The Statement of Work expressly provides that training of the Customer’s store and head-office staff is the responsibility of the Customer, with the Supplier only obliged to provide a train-the-trainer session and written user guides. This materially undermines a pleaded claim framed as the Defendant failing to train the Claimant’s staff generally.
nodes.quote: The Supplier shall provide a train-the-trainer session and written user guides only.

nodes.label: P28
nodes.text: By reason of the matters pleaded, the Claimant has suffered loss and damage.

nodes.label: By reason of the matters pleaded, the Clai
nodes.fulltext: By reason of the matters pleaded, the Claimant has suffered loss and damage.
nodes.quote: By reason of the matters aforesaid Meridian has suffered loss and damage.

nodes.label: Independent quantum expert supports that M
nodes.fulltext: Independent quantum expert supports that Meridian suffered at least some recoverable heads of loss in fact: wasted expenditure of £1.8m is consistent with accounting records, and some lost gross profit (around £1.3m) is attributable to platform defects, albeit far below the pleaded £4.2m.
nodes.quote: The pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement.

nodes.label: Termination/letter before action asserts t
nodes.fulltext: Termination/letter before action asserts that post-go-live failures caused substantial loss and that damages for wasted expenditure and loss of profit would be sought.
nodes.quote: Our client will be seeking damages, including wasted expenditure and loss of profit during the peak trading period, which are substantial.

nodes.label: Witness evidence describes serious stock-s
nodes.fulltext: Witness evidence describes serious stock-synchronisation problems, overselling, till issues and disruption during the busiest trading period, which is consistent with some loss or damage having been suffered.
nodes.quote: They caused a great deal of disruption for store teams during our busiest period.

nodes.label: Procurement witness states Meridian paid £
nodes.fulltext: Procurement witness states Meridian paid £1.8m under the contract and withheld the final £600k after post-go-live problems; this aligns with the wasted-expenditure figure later supported by the quantum expert.
nodes.quote: We withheld the final two milestone payments of £300,000 each — £600,000 in total — because of the problems we experienced after go-live.

nodes.label: Operational witness says the platform was
nodes.fulltext: Operational witness says the platform was only one factor among several; poor December trading was also caused by store broadband issues, a data-centre/network failure, the Lutterworth flood and weak market conditions. This materially weakens any broad attribution of all loss to the pleaded matters.
nodes.quote: Our trading in December 2024 was poor, but the platform was not the only reason.

nodes.label: P29
nodes.text: The Claimant claims wasted expenditure consisting of sums paid to the Defendant under the MSA in the amount of £1,800,000.

nodes.label: The Claimant claims wasted expenditure con
nodes.fulltext: The Claimant claims wasted expenditure consisting of sums paid to the Defendant under the MSA in the amount of £1,800,000.
nodes.quote: Wasted expenditure, being sums paid to TechFlow under the MSA: £1,800,000.

nodes.label: Priya Nair states Meridian paid TechFlow £
nodes.fulltext: Priya Nair states Meridian paid TechFlow £1.8 million in total under the contract and withheld the final two £300,000 milestone payments. This is direct factual evidence of the amount paid under the MSA.
nodes.quote: In total Meridian paid TechFlow £1.

nodes.label: The forensic accounting expert states that
nodes.fulltext: The forensic accounting expert states that the pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the MSA, and says there is no reason to challenge this element on a wasted-expenditure basis.
nodes.quote: The pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement.

nodes.label: The termination letter expressly states Me
nodes.fulltext: The termination letter expressly states Meridian will seek damages including wasted expenditure under the MSA. This supports that such a head of loss was asserted, though it is less probative than the witness and expert evidence on amount.
nodes.quote: Our client will be seeking damages, including wasted expenditure and loss of profit during the peak trading period, which are substantial.

nodes.label: TechFlow’s response denies liability and q
nodes.fulltext: TechFlow’s response denies liability and quantum in full, says Meridian accepted Phase 1, chose to proceed with go-live despite advice to defer, and relies on contractual limitation/cap arguments. It does not directly dispute that £1.8 million was paid, but challenges recoverability of the claim.
nodes.quote: Go-live decision.

nodes.label: Helena Vance says Meridian proceeded to go
nodes.fulltext: Helena Vance says Meridian proceeded to go-live despite TechFlow’s recommendation to delay and acknowledges some connectivity issues were Meridian-side. This may affect causation or recoverability of wasted expenditure, though it does not contradict the payment figure itself.
nodes.quote: I am Helena Vance, Programme Director at Meridian Retail Group plc.

nodes.label: P30
nodes.text: The Claimant claims loss of profit during the peak trading period of November and December 2024 in the amount of £4,200,000.

nodes.label: The Claimant claims loss of profit during
nodes.fulltext: The Claimant claims loss of profit during the peak trading period of November and December 2024 in the amount of £4,200,000.
nodes.quote: Loss of profit during the peak trading period of November and December 2024: £4,200,000.

nodes.label: The January 2025 termination letter assert
nodes.fulltext: The January 2025 termination letter asserts that platform failures during the peak trading period caused substantial loss and that Meridian would seek loss-of-profit damages during that period. This supports the existence of a pleaded profit-loss claim, but not the accuracy of the £4.2m figure.
nodes.quote: Our client will be seeking damages, including wasted expenditure and loss of profit during the peak trading period, which are substantial.

nodes.label: Helena Vance describes serious post-go-liv
nodes.fulltext: Helena Vance describes serious post-go-live stock-synchronisation and till problems during peak trading, which gives some factual basis for a profits claim arising in November–December 2024. However, she does not quantify loss at £4.2m.
nodes.quote: After go-live there were real problems with the stock-synchronisation module.

nodes.label: The forensic accounting expert directly ad
nodes.fulltext: The forensic accounting expert directly addresses the pleaded £4.2m loss-of-profit claim and concludes it is substantially overstated, with only about £1.3m incremental lost gross profit supportable and significant parts of the shortfall attributable to non-platform causes. It also notes clause 14 may exclude recovery for loss of profit altogether.
nodes.quote: Loss of profit 3.

nodes.label: Raymond Okafor says the platform was only
nodes.fulltext: Raymond Okafor says the platform was only one factor in poor December 2024 trading, identifying the Lutterworth flood, weak market conditions, store broadband issues, and a data-centre network failure as other significant causes. This materially undermines attribution of the full claimed amount to the platform.
nodes.quote: Our trading in December 2024 was poor, but the platform was not the only reason.

nodes.label: Priya Nair is not in a position to comment
nodes.fulltext: Priya Nair is not in a position to comment on technical performance and gives no support for the £4.2m quantum; her evidence is neutral on the amount and therefore leaves the pleaded figure unsubstantiated.
nodes.quote: Signed: ............................................ Name: Priya Nair Position: Head of Procurement, Meridian Retail Group plc Dated:

nodes.label: P31

nodes.label: The Claimant claims interest on sums found

nodes.quote: Further, Meridian claims interest on such sums as may be found due, pursuant to section 35A of the Senior Courts Act 1981, at such rate and for such period as the Court thinks fit.

nodes.label: E020 supports that some sums may be found
nodes.fulltext: E020 supports that some sums may be found due: it says wasted expenditure of £1.8m is supportable and some lost profit may be attributable to platform defects, which provides a possible principal sum on which interest could be sought.
nodes.quote: Wasted expenditure 2.

nodes.label: E018 supports that Meridian paid £1.8m und

nodes.quote: Defects 5.

nodes.label: E015 denies liability in full, disputes qu
nodes.fulltext: E015 denies liability in full, disputes quantum, and relies on clause 14 to exclude loss of profit and cap liability, so it challenges whether any sums will be found due at all.
nodes.quote: In any event, clause 14 of the MSA excludes liability for loss of profit and caps our client’s liability at the charges actually paid.

nodes.label: P32
nodes.text: The Claimant claims damages of approximately £6,000,000.

nodes.label: The Claimant claims damages of approximate
nodes.fulltext: The Claimant claims damages of approximately £6,000,000.
nodes.quote: PARTICULARS: (a)Wasted expenditure, being sums paid to TechFlow under the MSA: £1,800,000.

nodes.label: The forensic quantum expert identifies the
nodes.fulltext: The forensic quantum expert identifies the Claimant’s pleaded losses as £1.8m wasted expenditure plus £4.2m loss of profit, i.e. approximately £6m in total. This is the only direct evidence of the pleaded damages figure, but the same report materially reduces the supportable amount.
nodes.quote: I am instructed by Caldwell Pryce LLP to give my independent opinion on the quantum of the Claimant’s pleaded losses, namely wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 and loss of profit of £4,200,000.

nodes.label: Priya Nair states Meridian paid £1.8m unde
nodes.fulltext: Priya Nair states Meridian paid £1.8m under the contract and withheld £600,000, which supports one component of a damages claim, namely expenditure actually incurred, but does not support a total damages figure of about £6m.
nodes.quote: In total Meridian paid TechFlow £1.

nodes.label: Helena Vance describes serious post-go-liv
nodes.fulltext: Helena Vance describes serious post-go-live disruption and defects, which makes a damages claim generally plausible, but she gives no quantification approaching £6m.
nodes.quote: WITNESS STATEMENT OF HELENA VANCE 1.

nodes.label: The IT expert supports the existence of ge
nodes.fulltext: The IT expert supports the existence of genuine serious stock-synchronisation defects, which could underpin some damages claim, but does not quantify damages and also narrows aspects of the pleaded case.
nodes.quote: Notwithstanding the above, my opinion is that the Platform did contain genuine and serious defects in the stock-synchronisation module (defects D-001 and D-002 in the log).

nodes.label: TechFlow’s response disputes quantum entir
nodes.fulltext: TechFlow’s response disputes quantum entirely, says the loss-of-profit claim is grossly overstated, points to alternative causes such as the Lutterworth flood and market downturn, and relies on clause 14 as excluding loss of profit and capping liability at charges paid.
nodes.quote: In any event, clause 14 of the MSA excludes liability for loss of profit and caps our client’s liability at the charges actually paid.

nodes.text: The Claimant claims costs.

nodes.label: The Claimant claims costs.

nodes.quote: Claim No.

nodes.label: This claimant witness statement confirms M
nodes.fulltext: This claimant witness statement confirms Meridian has brought proceedings and sets out payment and withholding issues, but it does not prove or even expressly mention a pleaded claim for costs.
nodes.quote: Statement of Truth I believe that the facts stated in this witness statement are true.

nodes.label: The claimant has instructed a quantum expe
nodes.fulltext: The claimant has instructed a quantum expert in the litigation, which is consistent with active pursuit of the claim, but the report concerns damages and recoverability, not a claim for costs.
nodes.quote: Claim No.

nodes.label: The defendant's response denies liability
nodes.fulltext: The defendant's response denies liability and quantum and reserves a counterclaim, which would tend against any assumption that the claimant is entitled to recover costs, though it does not directly address a pleaded costs claim.
nodes.quote: Liability and quantum are both disputed.

nodes.label: The IT expert supports only part of the cl

paras.text: The Claimant ("Meridian") is a public limited company and operates a chain of approximately 180 retail stores in the United Kingdom together with an online sales channel.
The Defendant ("TechFlow") is a limited company which designs, supplies and implements retail software systems. The Agreement

By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 ("the MSA"), TechFlow agreed to design, build, configure and implement for Meridian a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform ("the Platform").
The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work ("the SOW") at Schedule 1, which set out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.
The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against milestones.

It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024, and that time was of the essence in respect of that date. The pre-contractual representation

At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, prior to entering into the MSA, TechFlow by its Sales Director Mr Daniel Frost orally represented to Meridian that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions ("the Representation"). Meridian entered into the MSA in reliance upon the Representation. The Representation was false: the Platform was incapable of supporting that volume. Breaches
In breach of the MSA, TechFlow delivered the Platform late. The Platform did not go live until 18 November 2024, some seven weeks after the contractual go-live date.
Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works. All delay was caused by TechFlow’s failure to allocate adequate and competent resources to the project.

On numerous occasions prior to go-live, Meridian warned TechFlow that the Platform was not ready and requested that go-live be deferred. TechFlow ignored those warnings and proceeded to go-live on 18 November 2024.

Following go-live, the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours during November and December 2024, causing widespread till failures and an inability to process sales across Meridian’s store estate.

The Platform contained numerous defects, including critical (Severity 1) failures in the stock-synchronisation module which caused stores to display and sell against incorrect stock figures. By reason of those defects the Platform was not of satisfactory quality and was not reasonably fit for its purpose.
Meridian did not accept the Platform or any part of it, and no acceptance or sign-off was given by Meridian at any time.
Further, TechFlow failed to provide adequate training to Meridian’s staff in the use of the Platform. Loss and damage

By reason of the matters aforesaid Meridian has suffered loss and damage. PARTICULARS: (a)Wasted expenditure, being sums paid to TechFlow under the MSA: £1,800,000. (b)Loss of profit during the peak trading period of November and December 2024: £4,200,000.
Further, Meridian claims interest on such sums as may be found due, pursuant to section 35A of the Senior Courts Act 1981, at such rate and for such period as the Court thinks fit.

14.3 Nothing in this Agreement limits liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence, for fraud, or for any other liability that cannot lawfully be excluded.
22. Entire agreement
22.1 This Agreement, together with its Schedules, constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior representations, statements or agreements, whether written or oral.

Thread: Go-live decision

Cc:
Priya Nair <p.nair@meridianretail.co.uk>; Gregory Manton <g.manton@techflow-solutions.co.uk>
Sent:
24 October 2024 16:52
Subject:
RECOMMENDATION: defer go-live to January 2025

Helena,
Following today’s readiness review, I want to put our recommendation in writing. The core build is in good shape and Phase 1 passed the bulk of UAT, but the loyalty module added under Change Order 3 is still being hardened and we have a handful of stock-sync issues we would like to close out under controlled conditions.
Our clear recommendation is to defer go-live to mid-January 2025, after the peak trading period. Going live on 18 November, immediately before Black Friday and Christmas, carries real risk and removes our contingency. We can hold the 18 November date if Meridian instructs us to, but we would be doing so against our advice.
Please let me know how you wish to proceed.
Marcus Reilly | Project Director | TechFlow Solutions

From:
Helena Vance <h.vance@meridianretail.co.uk>
To:
Marcus Reilly <m.reilly@techflow-solutions.co.uk>
Cc:
Priya Nair <p.nair@meridianretail.co.uk>
Sent:
24 October 2024 18:20
Subject:
RE: RECOMMENDATION: defer go-live to January 2025

Marcus,
Thank you, but the board has been clear that we need the new platform live for peak trading — that is the whole point of doing this now. Missing Black Friday and Christmas is not acceptable commercially.
Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned. I understand your team’s reservations and I am content to take that risk on Meridian’s side. Let’s get the remaining stock-sync items onto the snag list and press on.
Helena Vance | Programme Director | Meridian Retail Group

Helena, Raymond,

Thread: Q4 trading — distribution and market
From:
Raymond Okafor <r.okafor@meridianretail.co.uk>
To:
Helena Vance <h.vance@meridianretail.co.uk>
Cc:
Stephen Doyle <s.doyle@meridianretail.co.uk>
Sent:
8 December 2024 17:46
Subject:
December trading — a difficult picture

Helena,
A heads-up before the board call. December trading is well below plan and there are a few moving parts:
1) The Lutterworth distribution centre flooded on 3 December and was effectively out of action for around ten days. Roughly 40 stores had gaps on key lines through the first half of the month as a result. This is unrelated to the platform.
2) The market is soft — footfall is down across the sector and several competitors have gone early and deep on discounting. Our like-for-likes were already tracking behind in October.
3) On top of that, the platform stock-sync problems haven’t helped at the tills, though the teams have largely been working around them with manual checks.
I’d be wary of attributing the whole shortfall to the platform when the DC flood and the wider market are doing a lot of the damage. Happy to talk it through before the call.
Raymond

Dear Sirs
TechFlow Solutions Limited – Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 – RESPONSE TO LETTER BEFORE ACTION
We act for TechFlow Solutions Limited. Your client’s claim is denied in its entirety. Liability and quantum are both disputed.
Timetable and scope. The go-live date was a target, not a fixed date. It moved from 1 October to 18 November 2024 by reason of Change Order No. 3, signed by your client on 2 September 2024, which added a customer loyalty module that your client requested and which was not in the original scope. Your client’s assertion that it requested no change to scope is wrong and is contradicted by its own signed Change Order.
Go-live decision. Our client expressly recommended, in writing on 24 October 2024, that go-live be deferred to January 2025 to avoid the peak trading period. Your client’s Programme Director rejected that recommendation and instructed our client to proceed on 18 November 2024, accepting the associated risk. Your client cannot now complain of the consequences of its own decision.
Availability. The Platform was not "unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours". The most significant outage, on 25 November 2024, was caused by the failure of your client’s own network provider, Northgate Telecom, and not by the Platform.
Acceptance. Your client accepted Phase 1 by a signed Acceptance Certificate dated 12 November 2024.
Loss. Your client’s pleaded loss of profit is grossly overstated and ignores (a) the flooding of your client’s Lutterworth distribution centre in December 2024 and (b) the well-documented downturn in the retail sector during that quarter. In any event, clause 14 of the MSA excludes liability for loss of profit and caps our client’s liability at the charges actually paid.
Outstanding sums. Your client has wrongly withheld the final two milestone payments totalling £600,000, which remain due to our client. Our client reserves the right to counterclaim.
Yours faithfully
Harborne Quinn LLP

IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE
BUSINESS AND PROPERTY COURTS OF ENGLAND AND WALES
TECHNOLOGY AND CONSTRUCTION COURT (KBD)

B E T W E E N :
MERIDIAN RETAIL GROUP PLC
Claimant
- and -
TECHFLOW SOLUTIONS LIMITED
Defendant

WITNESS STATEMENT OF HELENA VANCE
1. I am Helena Vance, Programme Director at Meridian Retail Group plc. I make this statement from my own knowledge, save where I indicate otherwise. I had overall responsibility for the Platform implementation programme on Meridian’s side.
2. I was the main day-to-day contact with TechFlow. I worked closely with their Project Director, Marcus Reilly.
3. In around August 2024, our marketing team and the board decided we wanted a customer loyalty capability built into the new platform. I asked TechFlow to add a loyalty module. I accept this was not in the original Statement of Work. It was documented in Change Order No. 3, which my colleague Priya Nair signed on 2 September 2024, and which also moved the go-live date to 18 November 2024.
4. As we approached go-live, Marcus Reilly recommended in writing that we delay until January 2025, after the Christmas period. I decided that we should go live on 18 November 2024. The board was firm that we needed the platform live for peak trading, and I took the decision to proceed despite TechFlow’s advice. With hindsight, going live just before Black Friday was optimistic.
5. After go-live there were real problems with the stock-synchronisation module. Tills in some stores showed the wrong stock numbers, and on promotional lines we oversold in places. These problems were serious and took several weeks to bring under control. They caused a great deal of disruption for store teams during our busiest period.
6. There were also occasions when tills went down. I could not say for how long in total, and I understand some of the connectivity problems were to do with our own store broadband rather than the platform itself, but from where I sat it felt constant during the early weeks.
7. I considered that the platform we were given was not what we had paid for, and that is why Meridian decided to bring the relationship to an end.

Statement of Truth
I believe that the facts stated in this witness statement are true. I understand that proceedings for contempt of court may be brought against anyone who makes, or causes to be made, a false statement in a document verified by a statement of truth without an honest belief in its truth.

Position: Programme Director, Meridian Retail Group plc

I worked closely with their Project Director, Marcus Reilly. 3. In around August 2024, our marketing team and the board decided we wanted a customer loyalty capability built into the new platform. I asked TechFlow to add a loyalty module. I accept this was not in the original Statement of Work. It was documented in Change Order No. 3, which my colleague Priya Nair signed on 2 September 2024, and which also moved the go-live date to 18 November 2024. 4. As we approached go-live, Marcus Reilly recommended in writing that we delay until January 2025, after the Christmas period. I decided that we should go live on 18 November 2024. The board was firm that we needed the platform live for peak trading, and I took the decision to proceed despite TechFlow’s advice. With hindsight, going live just before Black Friday was optimistic. 5. After go-live there were real problems with the stock-synchronisation module. Tills in some stores showed the wrong stock numbers, and on promotional lines we oversold in places. These problems were serious and took several weeks to bring under control. They caused a great deal of disruption for store teams during our busiest period. 6. There were also occasions when tills went down. I could not say for how long in total, and I understand some of the connectivity problems were to do with our own store broadband rather than the platform itself, but from where I sat it felt constant during the early weeks. 7. I considered that the platform we were given was not what we had paid for, and that is why Meridian decided to bring the relationship to an end.

Statement of Truth I believe that the facts stated in this witness statement are true. I understand that proceedings for contempt of court may be brought against anyone who makes, or causes to be made, a false statement in a document verified by a statement of truth without an honest belief in its truth.

WITNESS STATEMENT OF RAYMOND OKAFOR
1. I am Raymond Okafor, Head of Retail Operations at Meridian Retail Group plc. I make this statement from my own knowledge save where otherwise indicated. I am responsible for the running of our store estate.
2. The new platform went live on 18 November 2024, immediately before our peak trading period. The first few weeks were very difficult for store teams, particularly because of stock figures being wrong at the tills on the new system.
3. That said, I should be straight about the wider picture. A number of the till outages in stores turned out to be caused by the broadband connections in the stores dropping out, rather than the platform itself. There was also a complete outage on 25 November that I am told was a network failure at our data centre provider, not the platform.
4. Our trading in December 2024 was poor, but the platform was not the only reason. Our distribution centre at Lutterworth flooded on 3 December 2024 and was effectively out of action for about ten days, which left around 40 stores short of stock on key lines in the first half of December. The market was also weak that quarter, with footfall down across the sector and heavy discounting by competitors.
5. I raised these points internally at the time, including in an email to Helena Vance on 8 December 2024, because I was concerned we should not put the whole of a bad December down to the platform when there were other significant factors.
6. None of this is to say the platform problems did not hurt us — they did, especially the stock-sync issues — but they were one factor among several.

Statement of Truth

Claim No. HT-2025-000231
Expert evidence (CPR Part 35) | IT / Software | 24 April 2026
Claim No. HT-2025-000231
IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE
BUSINESS AND PROPERTY COURTS OF ENGLAND AND WALES
TECHNOLOGY AND CONSTRUCTION COURT (KBD)

B E T W E E N :
MERIDIAN RETAIL GROUP PLC
Claimant
- and -
TECHFLOW SOLUTIONS LIMITED
Defendant

REPORT OF DR ALAN WHITFIELD
Independent Expert in Software Engineering and IT Systems
1. Introduction and instructions
1. I am Dr Alan Whitfield, BSc PhD CEng FBCS. I have 28 years’ experience in the design and assurance of enterprise retail IT systems. I am instructed by Caldwell Pryce LLP, solicitors for the Claimant, to give my independent opinion on (a) the availability of the Platform following go-live and (b) whether the Platform contained defects falling below the standard reasonably to be expected.
2. Availability of the Platform
2. I was provided with the Platform application and database logs, the network monitoring data and the defect log for the period 18 November 2024 to 10 January 2025. Having analysed the logs, my opinion is that the total Platform unavailability attributable to the Platform itself was approximately 6.2% of trading hours over the period.
3. That figure is materially lower than the figure of "more than 40% of trading hours" referred to in the pleadings. I have found no support in the logs for a figure of that order.
4. The single largest outage occurred on 25 November 2024 and lasted approximately five hours. In my opinion this outage was not caused by the Platform. The application and database remained healthy throughout; the cause was a loss of wide-area network connectivity at the Claimant’s data-centre provider (Northgate Telecom). A number of shorter in-store till interruptions were likewise attributable to in-store broadband, not to the Platform.
3. Defects
5. Notwithstanding the above, my opinion is that the Platform did contain genuine and serious defects in the stock-synchronisation module (defects D-001 and D-002 in the log). These were correctly categorised as Severity 1. They caused incorrect stock figures at the point of sale and overselling on certain lines, and persisted for approximately six weeks before being resolved.
6. In my opinion the presence of Severity 1 stock-synchronisation defects of this kind, persisting for around six weeks across a live store estate, falls below the standard reasonably to be expected of a competent supplier exercising reasonable skill and care.
4. Summary of opinion
7. In summary: (a) the Platform contained genuine and serious stock-synchronisation defects which fell below the expected standard; but (b) the pleaded allegation that the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours is not supported by the evidence, and the largest single outage was caused by the Claimant’s own network provider rather than the Platform.

Expert’s declaration
I understand that my duty is to help the court on matters within my expertise, and that this duty overrides any obligation to those instructing or paying me. I have complied with that duty. I have set out the substance of all facts and instructions which are material to the opinions I have expressed.
I confirm that I have made clear which of the facts stated in this report are within my own knowledge and which are not, and that the opinions I have expressed represent my true and complete professional opinions.
I believe that the facts I have stated in this report are true and that the opinions I have expressed are correct.

Signed: Dr Alan Whitfield
Dated: 24 April 2026

Claim No. HT-2025-000231
Expert evidence (CPR Part 35) | Forensic Accounting / Quantum | 24 April 2026
Claim No. HT-2025-000231
IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE
BUSINESS AND PROPERTY COURTS OF ENGLAND AND WALES
TECHNOLOGY AND CONSTRUCTION COURT (KBD)

B E T W E E N :
MERIDIAN RETAIL GROUP PLC
Claimant
- and -
TECHFLOW SOLUTIONS LIMITED
Defendant

REPORT OF FIONA GREENHALGH FCA
Independent Forensic Accountant
1. Introduction and instructions
1. I am Fiona Greenhalgh, a Fellow of the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales, and a partner in a forensic accounting practice. I am instructed by Caldwell Pryce LLP to give my independent opinion on the quantum of the Claimant’s pleaded losses, namely wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 and loss of profit of £4,200,000.
2. Wasted expenditure
2. The pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement. I have no reason to challenge this element on a wasted-expenditure basis.
3. Loss of profit
3. I have analysed the Claimant’s management accounts, EPOS sales data and prior-year comparatives for the November–December 2024 period. In my opinion the pleaded loss of profit of £4,200,000 is substantially overstated.
4. On the most supportable basis, the incremental lost gross profit capable of being attributed to the Platform’s defects is of the order of £1,300,000, not £4,200,000.
5. A material part of the Q4 sales shortfall is, in my opinion, attributable to causes unconnected with the Platform. In particular: (a) the flooding of the Claimant’s Lutterworth distribution centre in early December 2024, which disrupted stock to approximately 40 stores for around ten days; and (b) a sector-wide downturn in retail trading in the relevant quarter, evident in the Claimant’s own like-for-like data from October 2024 (i.e. before go-live). I estimate that approximately £1,500,000 of the claimed decline is attributable to these non-Platform factors, with the balance unsupported on the material I have seen.
4. Contractual limitation (matter for the Court)
6. I note, as a matter for the Court rather than for me, that clause 14 of the Master Services Agreement appears to exclude liability for loss of profit and to cap the Supplier’s liability at the charges paid (approximately £1,800,000). If that clause is effective, it would significantly affect the recoverability of the loss-of-profit claim regardless of my quantification.
5. Summary of opinion
7. In summary: the wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is supportable; the pleaded loss of profit of £4,200,000 is substantially overstated, with a supportable figure of around £1,300,000 before considering causation and the contractual limitation, and a significant part of the Q4 shortfall attributable to the distribution-centre flood and the wider market.

Expert’s declaration
I understand that my duty is to help the court on matters within my expertise, and that this duty overrides any obligation to those instructing or paying me. I have complied with that duty and will continue to do so. I believe that the facts I have stated in this report are true and that the opinions I have expressed are correct.

Signed: Fiona Greenhalgh FCA
Dated: 24 April 2026

MASTER SERVICES AGREEMENT
relating to the supply and implementation of a retail inventory and EPOS platform
Date: 14 March 2024
Parties: 
(1) MERIDIAN RETAIL GROUP PLC (company no. 03992145) of Meridian House, 40 Kingsway, London WC2B 6EX ("the Customer"); and
(2) TECHFLOW SOLUTIONS LIMITED (company no. 07781230) of Optimum Park, 12 Brindley Way, Reading RG1 8DF ("the Supplier").
1. Definitions and scope
1.1 The Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform ("the Platform") in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW").
1.2 The Supplier shall perform the Services with reasonable skill and care and in accordance with good industry practice.
2. Charges
2.1 The total charges for the Services are £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against the milestones set out in the SOW and as may be varied by agreement in writing.
3. Timetable and change control
3.1 The Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date stated in the SOW. The parties acknowledge that the implementation timetable is a planning estimate and may be adjusted through the change control procedure.
3.2 Any change to the scope, deliverables or timetable shall be agreed in writing by both parties by means of a signed Change Order. A signed Change Order varies this Agreement to the extent stated in it.
14. Limitation of liability
14.1 Subject to clause 14.3, the Supplier shall not be liable to the Customer, whether in contract, tort (including negligence) or otherwise, for any loss of profit, loss of revenue, loss of anticipated savings, or any indirect or consequential loss.
14.2 Subject to clause 14.3, the Supplier’s total aggregate liability arising under or in connection with this Agreement shall not exceed the total charges actually paid by the Customer under this Agreement in the twelve months preceding the event giving rise to the claim.
14.3 Nothing in this Agreement limits liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence, for fraud, or for any other liability that cannot lawfully be excluded.
22. Entire agreement
22.1 This Agreement, together with its Schedules, constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior representations, statements or agreements, whether written or oral. 
22.2 Each party acknowledges that it has not relied on, and shall have no remedy in respect of, any representation or statement not expressly set out in this Agreement (save in the case of fraud).
25. Governing law and jurisdiction
25.1 This Agreement is governed by the law of England and Wales, and the parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales.

REPORT OF DR ALAN WHITFIELD
Independent Expert in Software Engineering and IT Systems
1. Introduction and instructions
1. I am Dr Alan Whitfield, BSc PhD CEng FBCS. I have 28 years’ experience in the design and assurance of enterprise retail IT systems. I am instructed by Caldwell Pryce LLP, solicitors for the Claimant, to give my independent opinion on (a) the availability of the Platform following go-live and (b) whether the Platform contained defects falling below the standard reasonably to be expected.
2. Availability of the Platform
2. I was provided with the Platform application and database logs, the network monitoring data and the defect log for the period 18 November 2024 to 10 January 2025. Having analysed the logs, my opinion is that the total Platform unavailability attributable to the Platform itself was approximately 6.2% of trading hours over the period.
3. That figure is materially lower than the figure of "more than 40% of trading hours" referred to in the pleadings. I have found no support in the logs for a figure of that order.
4. The single largest outage occurred on 25 November 2024 and lasted approximately five hours. In my opinion this outage was not caused by the Platform. The application and database remained healthy throughout; the cause was a loss of wide-area network connectivity at the Claimant’s data-centre provider (Northgate Telecom). A number of shorter in-store till interruptions were likewise attributable to in-store broadband, not to the Platform.
3. Defects
5. Notwithstanding the above, my opinion is that the Platform did contain genuine and serious defects in the stock-synchronisation module (defects D-001 and D-002 in the log). These were correctly categorised as Severity 1. They caused incorrect stock figures at the point of sale and overselling on certain lines, and persisted for approximately six weeks before being resolved.
6. In my opinion the presence of Severity 1 stock-synchronisation defects of this kind, persisting for around six weeks across a live store estate, falls below the standard reasonably to be expected of a competent supplier exercising reasonable skill and care.
4. Summary of opinion
7. In summary: (a) the Platform contained genuine and serious stock-synchronisation defects which fell below the expected standard; but (b) the pleaded allegation that the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours is not supported by the evidence, and the largest single outage was caused by the Claimant’s own network provider rather than the Platform.

REPORT OF FIONA GREENHALGH FCA
Independent Forensic Accountant
1. Introduction and instructions
1. I am Fiona Greenhalgh, a Fellow of the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales, and a partner in a forensic accounting practice. I am instructed by Caldwell Pryce LLP to give my independent opinion on the quantum of the Claimant’s pleaded losses, namely wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 and loss of profit of £4,200,000.
2. Wasted expenditure
2. The pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement. I have no reason to challenge this element on a wasted-expenditure basis.
3. Loss of profit
3. I have analysed the Claimant’s management accounts, EPOS sales data and prior-year comparatives for the November–December 2024 period. In my opinion the pleaded loss of profit of £4,200,000 is substantially overstated.
4. On the most supportable basis, the incremental lost gross profit capable of being attributed to the Platform’s defects is of the order of £1,300,000, not £4,200,000.
5. A material part of the Q4 sales shortfall is, in my opinion, attributable to causes unconnected with the Platform. In particular: (a) the flooding of the Claimant’s Lutterworth distribution centre in early December 2024, which disrupted stock to approximately 40 stores for around ten days; and (b) a sector-wide downturn in retail trading in the relevant quarter, evident in the Claimant’s own like-for-like data from October 2024 (i.e. before go-live). I estimate that approximately £1,500,000 of the claimed decline is attributable to these non-Platform factors, with the balance unsupported on the material I have seen.
4. Contractual limitation (matter for the Court)
6. I note, as a matter for the Court rather than for me, that clause 14 of the Master Services Agreement appears to exclude liability for loss of profit and to cap the Supplier’s liability at the charges paid (approximately £1,800,000). If that clause is effective, it would significantly affect the recoverability of the loss-of-profit claim regardless of my quantification.
5. Summary of opinion
7. In summary: the wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is supportable; the pleaded loss of profit of £4,200,000 is substantially overstated, with a supportable figure of around £1,300,000 before considering causation and the contractual limitation, and a significant part of the Q4 shortfall attributable to the distribution-centre flood and the wider market.
